Were You Asked This?
Design Wikipedia Crawler

Design a distributed web crawler system that can crawl and store Wikipedia pages at scale using multiple machines or edge devices, with focus on coordination, deduplication, and efficient resource utilization.

Asked at:

Meta

G

glean

Lyft

Google

A distributed web crawler is a system that discovers, downloads, and stores web pages at scale so they can be analyzed, indexed, or archived. Think of it like a specialized version of what search engines do, but scoped to a particular site such as Wikipedia. Operators provide seed URLs, and thousands of workers (servers or edge devices) fetch pages, parse links, deduplicate content, and persist results.

Interviewers ask this because it tests your ability to coordinate a massive number of workers, prevent duplicate work, respect external constraints (robots.txt, rate limits), and keep storage and bandwidth under control. It also probes your understanding of exactly-once/at-least-once processing, sharding, backpressure, and fault tolerance—especially when the environment ranges from data center machines to untrusted edge/mobile devices without managed cloud services.

Community Solutions
Design Wikipedia Crawler
by Manav Agrawal
1
3
Common Functional Requirements

Most candidates end up covering this set of core functionalities

Users should be able to start a crawl from one or more seed URLs and define scope (e.g., only wikipedia.org, depth, and allowed content types).

Users should be able to reliably fetch and store pages with metadata (URL, fetch status, timestamp, ETag/Last-Modified) and extracted links.

Users should be able to avoid duplicate fetching of the same URL and detect duplicate or unchanged content to minimize bandwidth and storage.

Users should be able to monitor crawl progress, failures, and per-domain rate limits, and pause/resume or restart failed segments safely.

Common Deep Dives

Common follow-up questions interviewers like to ask for this question

How do you ensure exactly-once (or effective-once) processing per URL across 10k workers without a central bottleneck?

How will you perform large-scale deduplication (both URL-level and content-level) efficiently?

How do you enforce politeness (robots.txt compliance, per-host rate limits, and backoff) to avoid overloading Wikipedia?

If edge/mobile clients help crawl, how will you minimize network traffic and ensure trust in the data they return?

Relevant Patterns

Relevant patterns that you should know for this question

Multi-step Processes

Crawling is a pipeline: discover -> schedule -> fetch -> parse -> dedupe -> persist -> enqueue new URLs. Modeling this as a durable, multi-step workflow (with retries and compensations) is essential to maintain progress and correctness at scale.

Read More
Managing Long Running Tasks

A full-site crawl takes hours to weeks with intermittent failures, slow hosts, and backoff. Treating fetches as long-running tasks with leases, retries, and checkpointing ensures robustness without losing work.

Read More
Dealing with Contention

Hotspots emerge on shared domains and popular URLs. Per-host rate limiting, sharded ownership, and backpressure prevent thundering herds and protect external services while keeping throughput high.

Read More
Relevant Technologies

Relevant technologies that could be used to solve this question

Kafka

Kafka is a durable, horizontally scalable log ideal for the URL frontier and result streams. Partitions let you shard URLs by hash, support backpressure, and replay on failure, enabling at-least-once delivery with idempotent consumers.

Read More
ZooKeeper

ZooKeeper provides coordination primitives—leader election, service discovery, and lightweight locks/leases—useful for shard assignments, worker membership, and safely transferring ownership on failures without a single point of control.

Read More
Cassandra

Cassandra offers high write throughput and tunable consistency for crawl metadata (URL state machine, fingerprints, robots cache). Its wide-column model and time-based TTLs fit per-URL state, checkpoints, and dedupe indices at large scale.

Read More
Similar Problems to Practice

Related problems to practice for this question

Web Crawler

The core architecture—URL frontier, deduplication, politeness, distributed fetching—is identical. The Wikipedia scope narrows the domain but not the fundamental crawler mechanics.

Read
Practice
Job Scheduler

Assigning URLs to workers with leases, retries, and idempotent tasks mirrors distributed job scheduling patterns like Airflow or a durable execution engine.

Read
Practice
Rate Limiter

Per-host throttling and global backpressure replicate a distributed rate limiter’s design: token buckets, sharding by key (host), and consistent enforcement across many workers.

Read
Practice
Red Flags to Avoid

Common mistakes that can sink candidates in an interview

Single centralized queue or coordinator for all URLs

This creates a throughput bottleneck and a single point of failure. With 10k workers and billions of URLs, you need sharded ownership and partitioned scheduling to scale and remain resilient.

No robust deduplication or URL normalization strategy

Skipping canonicalization and a distributed seen-set leads to loops, massive duplicate fetches, and ballooning storage/bandwidth. It also undermines correctness metrics and progress reporting.

Ignoring robots.txt and per-host rate limits

Overloading Wikipedia or violating robots.txt is both unethical and operationally dangerous. It can get your crawler blocked and triggers systemic retries, failures, and wasted capacity.

Comment
Anonymous
Posting as woaidiyia
P
PrincipalPinkCattle431
Premium
• 4个月前

One of the Red Flags to avoid
Single centralized queue or coordinator for all URLs

Wikipedia has around 65M pages, why can't a stateless central service manage that much load?
It can horizontally scale as it is stateless, the real issue is DB, if each crawler crawls a page every 5s, that's
1 page/5s
for 10K crawlers = 2000 pages/
if each page has 100 links = 200K new urls/s

DB has to support writing these many URLs, DB can be sharded but this data is also highly skewed,
once a URL has been identified, going forward new urls are going to be very few, and pages don't change that much and if we can cache 64M urls and last fetched timestamp in memory our subsequent writes will be much lower.
I see upserting URLs to DB as a bottleneck but DB can be sharded or data can be cached and with batching even DB upserts can be fast.

The central service is not a optimal if you have billions of urls, but even then DB will be the bottleneck. M y question is what is the anti-pattern with having a stateless central service, and not considering queues because edge devices should not have access to queue directly, edge devices should also not have sharding logic embedded in them, so we do need a gateway/LB to route to any queue.

0
